common_name: Piracicaba Broccoli (Heat-Tolerant Brazilian Sprouting Broccoli for Warm Climates)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting to garden; in zone 9, start seeds in August–September for fall transplanting in September–October, targeting harvest December–March. Piracicaba was developed in Brazil for production in subtropical warm-winter conditions where standard heading broccoli (such as Packman or Marathon) struggles with heat-induced bolting and premature heading. Rather than forming a single large central head, Piracicaba produces an abundance of small, tender loose florets on branching side shoots over an extended harvest period — a sprouting habit similar to De Cicco broccoli but with dramatically better heat tolerance. In zone 9, Piracicaba can be harvested continuously through the mild winter rather than requiring a single-harvest timing, and it continues producing side shoots well into spring when standard broccoli bolts.

directions: Start seeds indoors; transplant to garden in fall for zone 9 winter harvest. Harvest loose florets continuously. Piracicaba Broccoli originated at the Universidade Estadual de Campinas (UNICAMP) and agronomic research stations near Piracicaba, a city in São Paulo state, Brazil — a major center of Brazilian tropical agricultural research. The variety was developed specifically to address the fundamental incompatibility between standard broccoli breeding (which targets cool-climate heading formation) and the subtropical conditions of southern Brazil and other warm-climate growing regions. Standard broccoli varieties were bred primarily in Europe and California's cool coastal fog belt, where temperature ranges precisely suited to head development allow reliable production; in warm climates, standard broccoli heats prematurely, forms loose, open heads of poor quality, or bolts before any marketable crop develops. Piracicaba's breeding solution was to select for a sprouting growth habit that produces continuously rather than forming a single marketable head — an approach that converts the temperature sensitivity of head formation from a problem into an advantage. Since the plant never forms a central head to be ruined by heat, there is no precise temperature window to miss, and the plant produces usable florets across a much broader temperature range. The florets of Piracicaba are small and tender, with a delicate texture superior to the sometimes-coarse texture of oversized main-head broccoli, and the continuous harvest over weeks or months provides far greater total yield per plant than single-harvest heading types in favorable conditions.
sources: Universidade Estadual de Campinas (UNICAMP) — Piracicaba broccoli variety development for subtropical production
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Heat-Tolerant Broccoli Varieties for Gulf South Fall Production
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Piracicaba broccoli variety notes and trial data

common_name: Belize Sage (Red-Stem Sage, Central American Tropical Salvia for Zone 9 Gardens)

planting.note: Propagate by stem cuttings (much faster to flowering than seed); Belize Sage is a tropical Salvia from the highlands of Guatemala, Belize, and southern Mexico — hardy to approximately 25–28°F when established, making it reliably perennial in zone 9 in protected microclimates and marginally hardy in zone 8b. In zone 9, Belize Sage behaves as a large, shrubby perennial that blooms nearly year-round in mild winters, with most intense flowering in late spring and fall. It tolerates partial shade, which makes it valuable in the Gulf Coast garden under open tree canopy or on the north side of structures where many salvias perform poorly. Hummingbirds visit the long red tubular flowers intensely during migration periods. Cut back hard after the coldest winter months to encourage vigorous new growth and renewed blooming.

directions: Propagate by cuttings in spring; plant in well-drained soil with afternoon shade in hottest climates. Cut back in late winter. Salvia miniata (Belize Sage, Red-Stem Sage) is a species of the large genus Salvia native to the highlands of Guatemala, Belize, and southern Mexico, where it grows as a shrubby perennial in forest margins and disturbed areas at moderate elevations. The common names 'Belize Sage' and 'Red-Stem Sage' both describe distinctive visual features of the plant: 'Belize Sage' references its Central American provenance, while 'Red-Stem Sage' describes the conspicuous red coloring of the stems, which sets the plant apart visually even when not in flower. The flowers are the primary ornamental draw: long, tubular, bright red blooms produced in upright racemes above medium green, somewhat velvety leaves, with a form and color that makes them exceptionally attractive to hummingbirds — the species has evolved the classic hummingbird-pollination syndrome of long tubular red flowers positioned and structured to deposit pollen on hovering birds. In zone 9, Salvia miniata's tolerance for partial shade — uncommon among the heat- and sun-loving Salvia genus — makes it one of the more versatile of the tropical sages. While full sun produces the most prolific flowering, the plant accepts several hours of afternoon shade without declining, allowing placement in spots where the intense Gulf Coast afternoon sun would stress less tolerant plants. This shade adaptability combined with year-round production in mild winters and hummingbird attraction makes Belize Sage a particularly useful plant for zone 9 gardens with mature tree canopy.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Salvia miniata species description and cultivation notes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical Salvia Species for Texas Zone 9 Gardens
Plant Delights Nursery — Salvia miniata culture and zone hardiness notes

common_name: Orange Sun Pepper (Fully Orange-Ripening Sweet Bell Pepper, Early and Productive)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Orange Sun is a fully orange-ripening bell pepper that reaches mature orange color rather than the halfway-ripened red-orange of some varieties that are harvested early. In zone 9, transplant March–April for spring production; a fall crop started from seed in July can extend production into October–November. The full orange color develops several weeks after the green harvest stage — allow peppers to reach full orange before picking for the sweetest, most nutritious fruit. Orange bell peppers are higher in certain carotenoids (beta-cryptoxanthin) than red or green bells, and the flavor at full orange ripeness is sweet and mild with none of the grassy notes of green-stage peppers. Plant in full sun with good drainage; consistent watering during fruit development prevents blossom end rot and promotes even development.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–80°F; transplant to full sun after last frost. Allow to fully color orange before harvesting for best flavor. Orange Sun is an F1 hybrid bell pepper variety selected for fully orange fruit at maturity — the distinctive bright orange color of an orange-ripe bell pepper rather than the mixed red-orange of incompletely ripened red varieties harvested early. Orange bell peppers as a category occupy an intermediate position in the bell pepper maturity and flavor spectrum: green peppers are harvested immature, red peppers are harvested fully ripe (or harvested orange-yellow then gassed), yellow and orange peppers represent distinct genetic selections that color differently from red-maturing types. True orange varieties like Orange Sun have the genetics to produce stable, uniform orange coloring at maturity — different from red varieties picked early, which produce a mottled, inconsistent orange appearance and inferior flavor. The walls of Orange Sun are thick and firm, excellent for fresh eating, roasting, and stuffing. The flavor at full orange ripeness is notably sweeter and more complex than green bells from the same plant, as the conversion of chlorophyll to carotenoids during ripening is accompanied by conversion of starches to sugars and development of aromatic compounds. Orange Sun is early-maturing for a bell pepper, reaching the green harvest stage approximately 70 days from transplanting and full orange ripeness a few weeks later — making it practical for the Gulf South's spring production window before summer heat limits continued setting.
sources: Harris Seeds — Orange Sun bell pepper variety description and performance notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Orange bell pepper variety comparison
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Sweet Pepper Variety Selection for Florida and the Gulf South

common_name: Yellow Tomatillo (Sweet Yellow-Ripening Tomatillo, Fruitier and Milder Alternative to Green)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; yellow tomatillo is grown identically to green tomatillo (Physalis ixocarpa) but ripens to yellow or golden rather than green, with a notably sweeter and less acidic flavor profile. In zone 9, transplant March–April; like all tomatillos, two plants are required for good fruit set (cross-pollination). Harvest when the papery husk is fully filled and just beginning to split — at this stage the fruit inside is plump, fully yellow, and at peak flavor. Overripe yellow tomatillo (husk fully split, fruit exposed) becomes starchy and flavor declines. Zone 9's long warm season allows multiple plantings from March through July; a July planting produces fall tomatillos in October–November when cooler temperatures improve flavor development.

directions: Start indoors; plant two or more for cross-pollination. Harvest when husk just fills completely and turns yellow-gold. Yellow Tomatillo (sometimes called 'miltomate amarillo' in its native Mexican context) is a color variant of the tomatillo (Physalis ixocarpa) that ripens to golden-yellow rather than the standard green, with a fundamentally different flavor profile: sweeter, less acidic, more fruity, and less pungent than the standard green tomatillo. The standard green tomatillo has an assertive, somewhat tart flavor with herbaceous, citrus-like notes that characterize the classic salsa verde (green sauce) of Mexican cuisine; the tartness and acidity are fundamental to the sauce's role of cutting through rich meats and cheeses. Yellow tomatillo has less of this characteristic tartness and more fruit-forward sweetness — a flavor profile that lends itself to milder, sweeter sauces and preparations where the aggressive acid of green tomatillo would be too dominant. Yellow tomatillo salsas tend to be used in different culinary contexts than green tomatillo salsas: they pair well with fish, light meats, and as a condiment where a sweeter, less assertive sauce is preferred. The plants grow identically to standard green tomatillo varieties — vigorous, spreading, 3–5 feet tall, requiring two plants for pollination — and have the same growing requirements. The papery husk that encloses the developing fruit is similar in color to green tomatillo husks (tan-brown with green veining) and turns slightly yellow-tan as the fruit inside ripens; the fully ripe fruit inside is a clear lemon-yellow to golden color.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Yellow tomatillo open-pollinated variety description
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Yellow tomatillo variety notes and flavor comparison
University of California Cooperative Extension — Tomatillo Color Variants and Flavor Profiles

common_name: King of the North Pepper (Heirloom Short-Season Sweet Bell Pepper with Zone 9 Fall Use)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; King of the North is an early-maturing sweet bell pepper (57–68 days from transplant) bred for cool, short-season climates where standard bell peppers ripen too slowly to produce before frost. In zone 9, where the problem is opposite (heat rather than cold), King of the North is best suited to fall planting (starts July, transplant August, harvest October–November) when cooling temperatures approach the 65–75°F range that bell peppers prefer for fruit set and maturation. The variety's short days-to-maturity allows it to complete ripening within the fall cool window. Harvest green or allow to fully ripen red for full sweetness. King of the North is an open-pollinated heirloom available from multiple seed sources and is notable for germinating and growing vigorously at lower temperatures than most other bell pepper varieties.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–80°F; transplant after last frost or in late summer for fall production. Harvest green or red. King of the North is an heirloom sweet bell pepper variety bred and popularized specifically for short-season, cool-climate gardens where standard bell pepper varieties (requiring 70–90+ days and substantial heat to develop fully) cannot be relied upon to ripen before fall frosts. The breeding goal of short-season bell peppers like King of the North is to compress the fruit development timeline while maintaining adequate size, wall thickness, and flavor — a challenging tradeoff since bell peppers that ripen quickly tend to be smaller and thinner-walled than late-maturing types. King of the North succeeds reasonably well at this balance: the fruits are large enough to be genuinely useful (3–4 inches, 3-lobed form), with walls adequate for stuffing and fresh use, and flavor that develops to full sweetness when ripened to red. The variety is open-pollinated and seed-saving is straightforward, making it a practical choice for seed-saving gardeners. In zone 9, the short days-to-maturity of King of the North are most valuable for the fall growing season rather than spring: a late-July start from seed produces plants that can be transplanted in late August and begin producing before October heat drops, allowing harvest through November in a climate where long-season varieties started at the same time might not mature before plants are compromised by cool weather. This fall-planting application in zone 9 mirrors King of the North's primary value in zone 3–5 gardens — speed of production in a constrained growing window.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — King of the North pepper heirloom open-pollinated variety documentation
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — King of the North short-season bell pepper variety notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Short-season sweet bell pepper variety comparison
